topic: Random-set sharp partial identification of direct and indirect treatment effects under sparse-network interference when the exposure mapping support is unknown but bounded in cardinality

open_problems.open_problem: Derive the sharp identified set for average direct and indirect effects when the true exposure mapping is unknown but its image has cardinality at most K.

source_refs.claim: They derive sharp bounds for direct and spillover effects under exposure-mapping misspecification, but instantiate fixed canonical exposure settings rather than an unknown finite-support map.
source_refs.author: Schroder, Oprescu, Feuerriegel, Kallus

source_refs.claim: Manski studies partial identification of treatment response with social interactions using weak restrictions on response functions.
source_refs.author: Manski

source_refs.claim: They characterize sharp identification regions through Aumann expectations of random sets in incomplete econometric models.
source_refs.author: Beresteanu, Molchanov, Molinari

source_refs.excerpt: The exact minimal number of units needed for strict containment is intentionally left open.

open_problems.why_unsolved: The closest web anchor gives sharp bounds for misspecified but fixed canonical exposure settings, while the prior CausalSmith network-exposure proposal fixes the candidate hidden-edge/support structure and leaves strict-containment regimes open. The missing regime is combinatorial: the exposure labels themselves are latent and chosen from all sparse-network-compatible maps with support size at most K, so sharpness must account jointly for random-set completion, label switching, and shared graph compatibility.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A theorem representing the sharp identified set as an Aumann expectation or support-function envelope over all K-valued sparse-network exposure maps, with constructive LP or convex-program formulas for direct and indirect effect bounds and a proof of sharpness.

open_problems.open_problem: Find the exact finite certificate family for infeasible cellwise network-exposure endpoints under capacitated hidden-edge constraints.

source_refs.excerpt: violated finite certificate from the associated capacitated b-matching / set-covering polytope
start with K_i=1 matching/cover certificates before claiming the fully capacitated b-matching case

open_problems.why_unsolved: The proposal names b-matching, cover, odd-set, and lifted-cover inequalities, and the reviewer explicitly asks for a smaller K_i=1 certificate before the fully capacitated case. What remains unresolved is not whether an LP can be written, but which finite inequalities are necessary and sufficient for endpoint feasibility when one exposure endpoint imposes signed demands that must be realized by one shared hidden network.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A necessary-and-sufficient certificate theorem for feasibility of signed exposure demands induced by a cellwise ADE endpoint, beginning with the K_i=1 case and extending or refuting completeness for capacitated b-matching constraints.

open_problems.open_problem: Prove whether shared hidden-edge compatibility makes unitwise exposure-propensity relaxations strictly nonsharp for network ADE bounds.

source_refs.excerpt: there exists an open set of finite experiments ... such that \Theta_I(P)\subsetneq \Theta_{\mathrm{cell}}(P)

open_problems.why_unsolved: The obstacle is joint realizability: a unitwise or cellwise relaxation can choose exposure-propensity endpoints independently, but the hidden edges that realize them must be one common graph satisfying degree and support constraints. The prior proposal marks strict containment as not yet proved and leaves the minimal witness size open.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A strict-containment theorem with an open-set finite-experiment witness, or a counterexample proving the cellwise relaxation is always sharp under the stated hidden-edge and exposure-support restrictions.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize when bounded-cardinality exposure support makes exposure-mapping misspecification statistically testable, despite broad impossibility results.

source_refs.claim: They prove no exposure-mapping specification test can uniformly outperform random rejection against larger exposure-map alternatives without further restrictions.
source_refs.author: Gao, Harshaw, Savje, Wang

source_refs.claim: Savje argues that exposures can define effects without assuming they fully capture the causal interference structure.
source_refs.author: Savje

source_refs.claim: They show correct network interference structures need not be unique and derive bias from using a misspecified network.
source_refs.author: Weinstein, Nevo

open_problems.why_unsolved: The current impossibility result is worst-case over broad nested exposure-map alternatives, while the CausalSmith topic imposes two extra restrictions: sparse network compatibility and exposure-image cardinality at most K. It is not known whether those restrictions create separation or still leave indistinguishable alternatives whose Type I and Type II errors exhaust power.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A dichotomy theorem giving either a uniformly consistent specification test under explicit sparsity, separation, and support-size assumptions, or a strengthened impossibility theorem covering every K-bounded sparse-network exposure-map class.

open_problems.open_problem: Obtain sharp joint bounds for decomposing a total policy effect into direct and indirect components when exposure labels are latent finite-support objects.

source_refs.claim: They define average direct and indirect effects under interference and show their sum matches an infinitesimal Bernoulli policy effect.
source_refs.author: Hu, Li, Wager

source_refs.claim: Hudgens and Halloran formalize direct, indirect, total, and overall effects under partial interference.
source_refs.author: Hudgens, Halloran
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

source_refs.claim: They identify effects with noncompliance and unknown interference while explicitly considering exposure-mapping misspecification.
source_refs.author: Hoshino, Yanagi
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

open_problems.why_unsolved: Direct and indirect decompositions in the cited literature rely on known treatment contrasts, known exposure summaries, or a fixed hierarchy of mappings. If the exposure support is latent and only cardinality-bounded, the total effect can remain bounded while the allocation between direct and indirect pieces is label-switching-sensitive and may not be componentwise sharp.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: Sharp lower and upper bounds for each component plus a compatibility theorem characterizing the joint set of direct-indirect pairs that attain a bounded total-effect decomposition.

open_problems.open_problem: Develop sparse-network asymptotic inference for sharp random-set bounds when interference is approximate rather than exactly local.

source_refs.claim: Leung replaces exact neighborhood interference with approximate neighborhood interference where distant treatments may have smaller nonzero effects.
source_refs.author: Leung

source_refs.claim: They show standard estimators can be consistent for a generalized estimand under limited but otherwise unknown interference, while confidence statements may fail.
source_refs.author: Savje, Aronow, Hudgens

source_refs.claim: They study random graph asymptotics for direct and indirect effect estimation under network interference.
source_refs.author: Li, Wager

open_problems.why_unsolved: Approximate-neighborhood and limited-unknown-interference asymptotics target point estimators or generalized estimands. Random-set bound endpoints over latent K-support maps introduce nonsmooth maxima over exposure classes while outcomes remain dependent through sparse neighborhoods, so neither standard CLTs nor existing partially identified interval inference apply directly.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A limit theorem for estimated support functions of the identified set, with valid confidence regions for bound endpoints under explicit degree, decay, and support-cardinality rates.

open_problems.open_problem: Construct the sharp identified set over equivalence classes of candidate networks and candidate exposure mappings, rather than assuming one correct network or one correct map is in hand.

source_refs.claim: They propose a network-misspecification-robust estimator that is unbiased if at least one candidate network correctly specifies interference.
source_refs.author: Weinstein, Nevo

source_refs.claim: They characterize limitations for estimating total treatment effects when the network driving interference is unknown.
source_refs.author: Yu, Airoldi, Borgs, Chayes

open_problems.why_unsolved: Robust estimators protect unbiasedness over finite candidate networks, and unknown-network work studies total effects, but neither gives the full sharp region for direct and indirect effects when many sparse networks and K-bounded exposure maps induce observationally equivalent exposure histories.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: An equivalence-class theorem and algorithm returning the exact envelope of all direct and indirect effects compatible with observed outcomes, treatment design, sparse candidate graphs, and exposure-support cardinality at most K.

literature_map:
Hudgens & Halloran (2008, JASA) give foundational direct, indirect, total, and overall effects under partial interference.
Manski (1993, ReStud) frames the reflection/reference-group problem; Manski (2013, Econometrics Journal) brings partial identification to treatment response with social interactions.
Bramoulle, Djebbari & Fortin (2009, Journal of Econometrics) provide peer-effect identification conditions in linear network models.
Aronow & Samii (2017, AOAS) establish the design/exposure-map/estimand framework under known exposure mappings.
Basse & Airoldi (2018, Sociological Methodology) show design-based limits under arbitrary or weakly structured interference.
Forastiere, Airoldi & Mealli (2021, JASA) and Forastiere et al. (2022, JMLR) develop observational-network direct/spillover estimation under neighborhood interference and generalized propensity scores.
Savje, Aronow & Hudgens (2021, Annals of Statistics) study average effects under unknown but limited interference.
Leung (2022, Econometrica) introduces approximate neighborhood interference for sparse growing networks.
Hu, Li & Wager (2022, Biometrika) define average direct and indirect effects whose sum matches an infinitesimal policy effect.
Yu, Airoldi, Borgs & Chayes (2022, PNAS) handle total treatment effects when the network is unknown, but not direct/indirect sharp bounds.
Hoshino & Yanagi (2024, JASA) consider unknown interference and noncompliance with exposure mappings of instruments.
Savje (2024, Biometrika) separates exposure mappings as estimand definitions from assumptions about the true interference structure.
Weinstein & Nevo (2026, Biometrics) separate network misspecification from mapping misspecification and robustify over candidate networks.
Schroder et al. (2026, arXiv) is the closest partial-ID anchor, giving sharp bounds for misspecified exposure mappings in fixed canonical settings.
Gao, Harshaw, Savje & Wang (2026, arXiv) show broad exposure-map specification testing is impossible without additional restrictions.
prior_proposal_map:
pid_network_random_set/v1 has no substantive proposal yet; only init/state files are present.
Closest direct prior is pid_network_exposure/v1: accepted field-tier shared-completion LP bounds for network ADEs.
Key network gaps: strict cellwise nonsharpness, exact capacitated certificate family, and numeric four-unit witness.
panel_approx_nbhd_interference/v1 supplies adjacent exposure-mapping/interference estimator-frontier gaps, but failed at derivation as generic operator algebra.
pid_mediation_proxy/v1 is adjacent for direct/indirect-effect partial-ID geometry and nonrectangular joint effect sets.
pid_ot_dte_marginal/v1 is adjacent random-set/support-function substrate for sharp partial-ID path sets and computable certificates.
pid_reversible_dynamic_spillover/v1 contributes bounded-spillover partial-ID threshold ideas, downgraded for lack of a non-generic symbolic frontier.
Study-pipeline gaps were reviewed as augmentation; their LATE and interval-regression questions were not promoted because they do not match the sparse-network exposure-support topic.
